narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data about thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds, focusing on factors that reliably signal bar success without considering gender or race. In your case, the three most influential factors were your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and third-year grade decile ranking. Your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive force, pushing the prediction toward success since your 3.40 is higher than the 3.24 average among students who passed the bar, showing the model views your prior college performance as a solid strength. Similarly, your LSAT score of 36 worked in your favor, as it was close to the 37.27 average for bar passers, helping to counterbalance other concerns. However, your third-year decile of 4 had the most significant negative pull, nudging the prediction toward failure because it falls well below the 5.91 average for those who succeeded, signaling to the model a potential dip in later academic momentum. While your upper-middle family income aligns with nearly half of bar passers and your choice to study full-time matches the vast majority of them, these didn't outweigh the year-three grades concern in the overall calculation. Compared to typical accepted students, your profile is strong in undergrad achievement and testing but lags in that key later decile, which often distinguishes those who thrive through law school rigor. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven read on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work to demonstrate upward trends. You might also explore post-baccalaureate options or gap-year experiences that highlight academic resilience, then reapply with fresh evidence. Many students in similar spots have strengthened their cases this way and succeeded—your solid foundations give you a great starting point for that journey.
